question.text: Under SEBI SAST Regulations 2011, a resolution plan approved under Section 31 of the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (IBC) is:

answer.text: Subject to a mandatory open offer if voting rights exceed 25%
Required to comply with voluntary offer provisions
Required to make an open offer within 90 days of NCLT approval
Exempt from the open offer obligation

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Acquisitions made pursuant to a resolution plan approved under Section 31 of the IBC are explicitly exempt from the mandatory open offer obligation under SAST 2011. This exemption facilitates the insolvency resolution process without the additional burden of an open offer. **The Trap:** Candidates might assume that all acquisitions exceeding 25% voting rights trigger an open offer. The IBC exemption is a critical exception that reflects the policy priority of enabling corporate rescue over shareholder protection in distressed situations. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series IX tests IBC-SAST interplay. The Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code 2016 also appears in NISM Series XII (Securities Markets Foundation). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** IBC resolution plans create unique acquisition opportunities — acquirers can gain control without open offer costs. But due diligence on the distressed asset must be thorough since normal information channels may be impaired.
